growth_records.medium: Artificial seawater mineral medium, +C-N regime (2 mmol/L C as glucose, 0 nitrate)

up.method: at 18C in +C-N the residual DOC stayed high (~2 mmol C/L, no depletion) -> net uptake ~0

growth_records.cite: Brauer VS et al. Competition and facilitation between the marine nitrogen-fixing cyanobacterium Cyanothece and its associated bacterial community. Frontiers in Microbiology (2015). DOI:10.3389/fmicb.2014.00795. [PMC4294207]
growth_records.snippet: Chemostats were run at a dilution rate of 0.2 d-1 ... contained either 0 or 100 umol L-1 NaNO3 and either 0 or 2 mmol L-1 carbon provided as glucose. The medium always contained 25 umol L-1 KH2PO4. ... In the +C-N treatment the DOC concentr
growth_records.notes: Chemostat steady state -> mu=D=0.2 d-1 = 0.00833 h-1 (CALCULATED mu=D). Doubling time td=ln2/mu=0.693/0.2=3.466 d=83.2 h. Glucose (DOC): feed 2 mmol C/L; at 18C +C-N residual stayed ~2 mmol C/L (not depleted) -> community volumetric net glucose-C uptake ~0. CO-CULTURE (cyanobacterium + heterotrophic bacteria); glucose consumed by heterotrophs, not attributable to a single organism. Full seawater base medium in Supplementary Table 1 (not in main text) -> medium_composition_stated=false. Biomass reported as cells/mL and biovolume, not gDW, so per-gDW rates not derivable.

growth_records.medium: Artificial seawater mineral medium, +C-N regime (2 mmol/L C as glucose, 0 nitrate)

up.method: phosphate depleted to <0.2 umol/L from 25; D*(25-0.2)=0.2*24.8=4.96 umol/L/d

growth_records.cite: Brauer VS et al. Competition and facilitation between the marine nitrogen-fixing cyanobacterium Cyanothece and its associated bacterial community. Frontiers in Microbiology (2015). DOI:10.3389/fmicb.2014.00795. [PMC4294207]
growth_records.snippet: In the +C-N treatment ... was only mildly depleted to 1.4 mmol C L-1 at 23C ... at 23 and 28C phosphate was depleted to <0.2 umol L-1 within 1 to 10 days.
growth_records.notes: mu=D=0.2 d-1=0.00833 h-1 (CALCULATED). td=83.2 h. Glucose-C uptake CALCULATED as chemostat mass balance D*(Cin-Cout)=0.2*(2.0-1.4)=0.12 mmol C/L/d (=0.02 mmol glucose/L/d, glucose=6 C). Phosphate uptake CALCULATED 0.2*(25-0.2)=4.96 umol P/L/d. Volumetric community rates (co-culture, cyanobacterium+heterotrophs); not per gDW. Nitrate absent (-N regime, diazotrophic N2 fixation). Seawater base in Suppl Table 1 not in text.

growth_records.medium: Artificial seawater mineral medium, +C-N regime (2 mmol/L C as glucose, 0 nitrate)
growth_records.cite: Brauer VS et al. Competition and facilitation between the marine nitrogen-fixing cyanobacterium Cyanothece and its associated bacterial community. Frontiers in Microbiology (2015). DOI:10.3389/fmicb.2014.00795. [PMC4294207]
growth_records.snippet: In the +C-N treatment ... to 0.9 mmol C L-1 at 28C ... at 23 and 28C phosphate was depleted to <0.2 umol L-1 within 1 to 10 days.
growth_records.notes: mu=D=0.2 d-1=0.00833 h-1 (CALCULATED). td=83.2 h. Glucose-C uptake CALCULATED D*(Cin-Cout)=0.2*(2.0-0.9)=0.22 mmol C/L/d. Phosphate uptake 0.2*(25-0.2)=4.96 umol P/L/d. Community volumetric (co-culture). Not per gDW.

growth_records.medium: Artificial seawater mineral medium, +C+N regime (2 mmol/L C as glucose, 100 umol/L nitrate)

up.method: nitrate depleted to very low from 100 umol/L; ~0.2*(100-0)=20 umol/L/d

growth_records.cite: Brauer VS et al. Competition and facilitation between the marine nitrogen-fixing cyanobacterium Cyanothece and its associated bacterial community. Frontiers in Microbiology (2015). DOI:10.3389/fmicb.2014.00795. [PMC4294207]
growth_records.snippet: in the +C+N treatment the DOC concentration was strongly depleted to 0.4 mmol C L-1 at 18C ... Nitrate concentrations were diminished to very low levels from the first day ... At 18C phosphate concentrations remained high at ~10 umol L-1.
growth_records.notes: mu=D=0.2 d-1=0.00833 h-1 (CALCULATED). td=83.2 h. Glucose-C uptake CALCULATED D*(2.0-0.4)=0.32 mmol C/L/d. Nitrate uptake ~0.2*(100-~0)=20 umol N/L/d. Phosphate uptake 0.2*(25-10)=3.0 umol P/L/d (18C, P not fully depleted). Community volumetric (co-culture); not per gDW.

growth_records.medium: Artificial seawater mineral medium, +C+N regime (2 mmol/L C as glucose, 100 umol/L nitrate)

up.method: nitrate depleted to very low from 100 umol/L; ~0.2*(100-0)=20 umol/L/d

growth_records.cite: Brauer VS et al. Competition and facilitation between the marine nitrogen-fixing cyanobacterium Cyanothece and its associated bacterial community. Frontiers in Microbiology (2015). DOI:10.3389/fmicb.2014.00795. [PMC4294207]
growth_records.snippet: in the +C+N treatment the DOC concentration was strongly depleted ... to <0.15 mmol C L-1 at 23 and 28C ... at 23 and 28C phosphate was depleted to <0.2 umol L-1.
growth_records.notes: mu=D=0.2 d-1=0.00833 h-1 (CALCULATED). td=83.2 h. Glucose-C uptake CALCULATED D*(2.0-0.15)=0.37 mmol C/L/d. Nitrate uptake ~20 umol N/L/d. Phosphate uptake 0.2*(25-0.2)=4.96 umol P/L/d. Community volumetric (co-culture); not per gDW.

sec.units: mmol H2/mg Chl (verbatim; time basis unclear)
sec.method: cited in review from Masukawa et al. 2002

growth_records.cite: Agarwal et al. Cyanobacteria as a Promising Alternative for Sustainable Environment: Synthesis of Biofuel and Biodegradable Plastics. Frontiers in Microbiology (2022). DOI:10.3389/fmicb.2022.939347. [PMC9325326]
growth_records.snippet: Cyanothece sp. ATCC 51142 | Oxygenic photo-autotrophic | Chemostat culture-ASP-2 medium, Nitrogen deprivation, Continuous white light illumination | Variable | Hydrogen | 400 mmol H2/mg Chl. H | Disruption of uptake hydrogenase gene
growth_records.notes: SECONDARY value cited within REVIEW (Agarwal et al. 2022) from Masukawa et al. 2002. Growth rate listed as 'Variable' -> growth_rate null. H2 yield '400 mmol H2/mg Chl. H' recorded verbatim; time basis and 'mg Chl' normalization ambiguous as printed in the review table (likely a per-Chl production value); flagged low confidence, units not sanitized/invented. Chemostat, ASP-2 medium, N deprivation, uptake-hydrogenase knockout.

growth_records.medium: Photo-autotrophic medium (CO2 as carbon source, nitrate as N source; full composition not stated, from Dechatiwongse et al. 2015)
growth_records.cite: Zhang, Dongda et al. Dynamic modelling of high biomass density cultivation and biohydrogen production in different scales of flat plate photobioreactors. Biotechnology and Bioengineering (2015). DOI:10.1002/bit.25661. [PMC4975697]
growth_records.snippet: mu_max,a (hr-1) 0.255 ... temperature in the current experimental research is fixed at 35C. A previous study presents a dynamic model for the simulation of Cyanothece sp. photo-autotrophic growth under nitrogen and carbon (CO2) sufficiency.
growth_records.notes: Prokaryote (nitrogen-fixing cyanobacterium). Value is the maximum specific growth rate mu_max,a=0.255 h-1 for photo-AUTOTROPHIC growth, obtained by parameter estimation (4th-order orthogonal-collocation fit) of laboratory batch photobioreactor data (experimental setup from Dechatiwongse et al. 2015; Zhang et al. 2015), Table I. Doubling time computed td=ln2/mu=0.693/0.255=2.72 h. Not a directly measured instantaneous rate; it is a model-fitted kinetic maximum, so treated as calculated/model-derived. Temperature 35C stated explicitly. Medium composition not given in this paper (references source paper).

growth_records.medium: Photo-heterotrophic medium (glycerol as additional carbon source replacing CO2; nitrate as limiting N source; full composition not stated, from Dechatiwongse et al. 2015)

up.units: mmol/gDW (Yc/x, glycerol consumed per biomass formed)
up.method: parameter estimation / model fit to batch PBR data

sec.method: parameter estimation / model fit to batch PBR data

growth_records.cite: Zhang, Dongda et al. Dynamic modelling of high biomass density cultivation and biohydrogen production in different scales of flat plate photobioreactors. Biotechnology and Bioengineering (2015). DOI:10.1002/bit.25661. [PMC4975697]
growth_records.snippet: Parameters in the hydrogen production model: mu_max,a (h-1) 0.332; mu_d,a 0.00716; YN/X (mg g-1) 492.7; YH/X (mL g-1 h-1) 14.20; Yc/x (mmol g-1) 20.454; Yc (mmol g-1 h-1) 0.0301. CO2 is replaced by glycerol ... to provide electrons for hydr
growth_records.notes: Prokaryote cyanobacterium, photo-HETEROTROPHIC condition (glycerol carbon source). All numbers are model parameters (Table II) re-estimated by fitting laboratory batch photobioreactor data (Dechatiwongse et al. 2015). mu_max,a=0.332 h-1 (enhanced vs autotrophic 0.255 after glycerol supply); td=ln2/0.332=2.09 h. YH/X=14.20 mL H2 g-1 h-1 is a specific (per-biomass) H2 production rate. Yc/x=20.454 mmol glycerol g-1 biomass; Yc=0.0301 mmol g-1 h-1 maintenance glycerol. YN/X=492.7 mg nitrate-N g-1 biomass. These are fitted (not directly measured) so is_experimental=false. Intro also cites (from Dechatiwongse et al. 2015 / Tamburic et al.) a biohydrogen productivity of 2.33 mL H2 L-1 h-1 for Cyanothece sp. in a 1 L flat-plate PBR (width 0.025 m) - that value is CITED from another paper, not measured here.

up.method: 1.3% CO2-Ar sparge; carbon source is fixed CO2

sec.method: in situ polarographic dO2 sensor; steady-state photosynthetic O2 evolution
in situ dH2 sensor; no H2 detected at N-replete steady state

growth_records.cite: Matthew R. Melnicki et al. Sustained H2 Production Driven by Photosynthetic Water Splitting in a Unicellular Cyanobacterium. mBio (2012). DOI:10.1128/mBio.00197-12. [PMC3419522]
growth_records.snippet: NH 4 + -limited chemostat cultures were established at a 5.5-liter working volume, 30 C, and pH 7.5 using modified low-N ASP-2 containing 0.75 mM NH 4 Cl delivered at a 0.05 h-1 dilution rate... Cyanothece 51142 evolved O 2 at a rate of 350
growth_records.notes: Photoautotrophic diazotroph. Steady-state chemostat: mu = D = 0.05 h^-1; doubling time = ln2/0.05 = 13.86 h. Rates reported per mg chlorophyll (paper's native unit). Approx conversion to per-gDW at steady state using Chl 1.08 ug/mL and total DCW 181 ug/mL => 5.97 mg Chl/gDW; O2 350 umol/mgChl/h x 5.97e-3 mgChl/ug? -> 350 x 5.97 = 2089 umol/gDW/h = 2.09 mmol/gDW/h (valid only at steady state; chlorophyll changes during N deprivation). CO2 is the carbon source (fixed) but no numeric uptake rate given.

growth_records.medium: Modified low-N ASP-2, N-deprived (NH4+ influx halted), 1.3% CO2-Ar sparge

sec.method: in situ dH2 sensor; net rate via first-order removal coefficient 74.320 /h

sec.units: umol/mg Chl/h (median, late/dampened phase at 100 h)

sec.method: in situ dO2 sensor; net rate via first-order removal coefficient 55.024 /h

sec.units: umol/mg Chl/h (median, late/dampened phase at 100 h)

growth_records.cite: Matthew R. Melnicki et al. Sustained H2 Production Driven by Photosynthetic Water Splitting in a Unicellular Cyanobacterium. mBio (2012). DOI:10.1128/mBio.00197-12. [PMC3419522]
growth_records.snippet: H 2 ... production rates steadily accelerated to 400 umol mg Chl-1 h-1 over the next 24-h period; at the same time, the rate of O 2 evolution began to gradually decline until it reached a stable level of ~100 umol mg Chl-1 h-1 ... median H2
growth_records.notes: N-deprivation (halted NH4+ influx) drives dinitrogenase-catalyzed H2 evolution concomitant with photosynthetic O2. Peak H2 ~400 umol/mgChl/h at ~24 h; O2 declines to ~100; late dampened medians H2=125, O2=90 umol/mgChl/h. Rates are net (dissolved-gas sensor slopes with reported first-order removal coefficients). No growth rate in this N-deprived batch (biomass not increasing; N-starved). Units per mg chlorophyll.

growth_records.medium: Modified low-N ASP-2, N-deprived, pure Ar sparge (no CO2)

sec.units: umol/mg Chl/h (detectable, steadily increasing over first 24 h; second low-level oscillation 48-90 h; single peak value not separately tabulated)
umol/mg Chl/h (low initially, recovered from 4 h, positively correlated with H2)

growth_records.cite: Matthew R. Melnicki et al. Sustained H2 Production Driven by Photosynthetic Water Splitting in a Unicellular Cyanobacterium. mBio (2012). DOI:10.1128/mBio.00197-12. [PMC3419522]
growth_records.snippet: removing CO 2 from the sparge gas... H 2 became detectable shortly after N deprivation, with rates which also steadily increased over the first 24 h... total carbohydrate levels... reaching 45 mg liter-1 in 6 h with Ar
growth_records.notes: Pure-Ar (CO2-free) N-deprivation variant. H2 and O2 dynamics described but discrete peak rates not separately tabulated in text (shown in Fig 1b), so numeric rate left null to avoid invention. Carbohydrate accumulation 45 mg/L in 6 h. Mass balance: carbohydrate consumption accounts for at best 40% (Ar-only) / 51% (Ar-CO2) of electrons for H2.

growth_records.medium: Chemostat cells in sealed tubes, Ar atmosphere

sec.units: umol/mg Chl/h (derived 72-h average from 4.46 mmol/mgChl endpoint)
sec.method: GC-TCD endpoint of tube headspace; accumulation 4.46 +/- 0.58 mmol H2/mg Chl over 72 h

sec.units: umol/mg Chl/h (derived 72-h average from 2.65 mmol/mgChl endpoint)
sec.method: GC-TCD endpoint; accumulation 2.65 +/- 0.14 mmol O2/mg Chl over 72 h

growth_records.cite: Matthew R. Melnicki et al. Sustained H2 Production Driven by Photosynthetic Water Splitting in a Unicellular Cyanobacterium. mBio (2012). DOI:10.1128/mBio.00197-12. [PMC3419522]
growth_records.snippet: after 72 h of continuous illumination with white light under an Ar atmosphere, Cyanothece 51142 cells evolved 4.46 +/- 0.58 mmol H 2 mg Chl-1 and 2.65 +/- 0.14 mmol O 2 mg Chl-1, implying PSII activity as well as a possible O 2 tolerance
growth_records.notes: Endpoint accumulation (not instantaneous rate). Derived average rates: H2 = 4.46 mmol/mgChl / 72 h = 0.0619 mmol/mgChl/h = 61.9 umol/mgChl/h; O2 = 2.65/72 = 0.0368 mmol/mgChl/h = 36.8 umol/mgChl/h. These are lower-bound time-averages (rates were non-constant). Endpoint measured by GC-TCD after HCl quench.
